description: Crime requires characters defined by their relationship to criminal activity, moral compromise as engine, and crime as the primary driver of every major plot development.

character_constraints.description: A character defined by their relationship to criminal activity — criminal, cop, victim, or someone drawn in.
An opposing force from within or outside the criminal world — rival, law enforcement, or betrayer.
The human cost of crime — individuals or community affected by criminal activity.
The institutional pressure of justice — present even when not the protagonist.

relationship_constraints.description: Direct opposition rooted in criminal activity — pursuit, rivalry, or betrayal.
A relationship in which a moral line is crossed — complicity, coercion, or seduction into crime.

place_constraints.description: Safe houses, target buildings, neutral ground — the geography of criminal operations.
Precinct, courtroom, or interrogation room — the institutional counterweight.
The location where criminal and legal worlds collide in the climax.

object_constraints.description: Drugs, stolen goods, laundered money — the physical substance of the crime.
Physical proof that could convict or exonerate — its control drives plot.
Tools of violence that escalate stakes when they appear.
Money or valuables that represent the motive and reward of crime.

element_rules.description: Crime must be the primary engine of every major plot development — not merely background or setting.
element_rules.testable_condition: At least 3 major timeline transitions are directly caused by criminal activity or its consequences.

element_rules.description: A moral compromise scene and a reckoning scene are both mandatory — the protagonist must cross a line and face consequences.
element_rules.testable_condition: The timeline contains a 'compromises' transition and a subsequent 'reckons' or 'faces consequences' transition.
